retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Ameloblastic fibroma may share the same features histologically, such as small cords and islands of ameloblastic epithelium that's just two cells thick containing dense collagenous stroma which is often immature. It can also occasionally contain cementum or dentin production as well as stellate reticulum. The main feature of ameloblastic fibroma is that the stroma is more primitive and it should not metastasize.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise Only From Odontogenic Epithelium Without Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme
retrieved_docs.content: While ameloblastic fibroma contains thin strands of odontogenic epithelium in the background of embryonic connective tissue, it may also show focal areas of hyalinization and calcification. [23] Finally, odontoma can be either compound or complex. Compound odontoma consists of enamel, dentin, and pulp surrounded by follicular connective tissue. They are usually arranged in an orderly manner, similar to normal teeth. While in complex odontoma, tissue arrangement is totally different from normal teeth. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic tumors that arise from ectomesenchyme with or without included odontogenic epithelium:
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis is challenging in small biopsies as it can mimic other head and neck tumors. Finally, odontogenic sarcoma and carcinosarcoma in which epithelial component is similar to ameloblastoma, or ameloblastic fibroma. However, the mesenchymal component shows malignant features. With recurrence, the stromal component increases, and the epithelial component diminishes. If dysplastic dentin is present in ameloblastic fibrosarcoma, then it is called ameloblastic fibrodentinosarcoma. While if it additionally shows foci of dysplastic enamel protein, it is called ameloblastic fibro-odontosarcoma. Moreover, if both carcinomatous and malignant spindle cell components are present, it is then odontogenic carcinosarcoma. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise Only From Odontogenic Epithelium Without Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme
retrieved_docs.content: Odontogenic tumors that arise from both odontogenic epithelium and odontogenic ectomesenchyme, with or without dental hard tissue formation: Odontogenic ghost cell tumor (calcifying odontogenic cyst) epithelium usually resembles ameloblastoma with an outer layer of palisaded columnar cells and an inner layer of stellate reticulum. Spherical calcification and hyalinized materials can also be seen. Some of the eosinophilic epithelial cells may lack nuclei, which are called “ghost cells.” [21] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise Only From Odontogenic Epithelium Without Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme
retrieved_docs.content: Acanthomatous subtype in which extensive squamous metaplasia of the central core is present. It is often associated with keratin formation. It may be confused as squamous cell carcinoma. Granular cell type is a clinically aggressive type in which tumor cells' cytoplasm shows abundant eosinophilic granules. The desmoplastic type is characterized by densely collagenized stroma. It is clinically more aggressive with a higher rate of recurrence. Finally, the basal cell type is the least common type with a nest of basaloid cells, which resemble basal cell carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoprogenitor Cells -- Histochemistry and Cytochemistry
retrieved_docs.content: Several research groups have studied the development of osteoprogenitor cells. A study suggests that the perichondrial Thy-1–positive cells demonstrate potential osteogenic activity and participate in osteoblast formation during endochondral ossification. Another study shows intense alkaline phosphatase activity 2 weeks after osteogenic induction and the presence of mineralized nodules. The expression of bone sialoprotein, dentin matrix protein-1, and osteocalcin increases due to ALP proliferation. As confirmed by flow cytometry, 55 to 65% of cells display the cell-surface markers Sca1+ and Thy1+ in vitro expansion for an alpha-SMA-GFP positive population. The alpha-SMA–GFP-positive population also exhibits higher proliferative and osteogenic probabilities compared to an alpha-SMA–GFP-negative population. [8] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic tumors that arise from ectomesenchyme with or without included odontogenic epithelium:
retrieved_docs.content: Histological features of odontogenic fibroma show stellate fibroblast with islands of odontogenic epithelium. They may also show spherical or diffuse calcification. It may be central or peripheral. [19] [42] While odontogenic myxoma shows abundant myxoid stroma with haphazardly arranged spindle cells. Abundant collagen fibers can be seen in some patients, and these lesions are considered to be fibromyxoma. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Osteosarcoma
retrieved_docs.content: Osteosarcoma is of mesenchymal origin and exhibits proliferation of osteoblastic precursors, which produce osteoid and immature bone. Neoplastic cells will show anaplasia, filling of marrows space, and erosion of trabecular bone. They can be histologically classified depending on the extracellular matrix cells produced: osteoblastic, chondroblastic, and fibroblastic. The osteoblastic subtype is most commonly observed in the jaws. [36] The precise histology can be variable and can cause diagnostic difficulty, and often multiple biopsies are taken before a diagnosis can be made.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise From Both Odontogenic Epithelium and Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme, With or Without Dental Hard Tissue Formation
retrieved_docs.content: Odontogenic ghost cell tumor (calcifying odontogenic cyst) usually appears as well-circumscribed unilocular translucency with flecks of radio-opacity and may be associated with unerupted canine. [20] While ameloblastic fibroma can be either a unilocular or multilocular lesion. Compound odontoma is usually a unilocular lesion with multiple radiopaque miniature teeth-like structures. Complex odontoma is usually a solid radiopaque mass surrounded by a zone of translucency (Fig. 5) . Developing odontoma may be translucent and mostly associated with unerupted teeth. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic tumors that arise from ectomesenchyme with or without included odontogenic epithelium:
retrieved_docs.content: Primary intraosseous carcinoma usually has the features of high-grade squamous cell carcinoma. Ghost cell odontogenic carcinoma also shows features of ameloblastoma such as peripheral palisading and reverse polarization along with the presence of ghost cells. NOS Sclerosing odontogenic carcinoma shows extensive sclerosis within connective tissue stroma and bland epithelial cell that does not show keratin differentiation. Mitoses and nuclear pleomorphism are rare. However, perineural and perivascular invasion and the invasion of the surrounding musculature and cortical bones are quite common. [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The pulp can be anatomically divided into four zones: the odontoblastic layer, mesenchymal zone, cell-rich zone, and the core. The odontoblasts are aligned in the periphery of the pulp and produce secondary or tertiary dentin; the second layer is mostly acellular and has loose connective tissue with nerve fibers and small blood vessels; the adjacent area is more cellular and has a rich vascular and nerve supply, similar to the central core located in the center of the pulp chamber.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic tumors that arise from ectomesenchyme with or without included odontogenic epithelium:
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant odontogenic tumors: In ameloblastic carcinoma, both primary lesion and metastases show cytological malignancy features, which is the main difference from metastasizing (malignant) ameloblastoma. [27] [28] While clear cell odontogenic carcinoma may appear in two forms, either monophasic. Only clear cells are present or biphasic, in which clear cells are present along with cells containing eosinophilic cytoplasm. It may also show some ameloblastoma features, such as central cystic changes with squamous proliferation and peripheral palisading appearance. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Odontogenic tumors that arise from ectomesenchyme with or without included odontogenic epithelium:
retrieved_docs.content: Lastly, cementoblastoma resembles osteoblastoma and osteoid osteoma, except that it is usually in continuity with the cemental layer of the roots. Eosinophilic matrix rimmed by cementoblast cells. It is relatively acellular in the periphery with a more vascular and mineralized center. The center usually shows multinucleated giant cells with reversal lines which indicates extensive remodeling. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that this malignant condition involves the upper jaw and reflects a neoplastic process affecting both bone and connective tissue structures in that region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding that indicates the presence of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue samples signals that cancerous cells have invaded the bone, serving as an important diagnostic marker for confirming bone malignancy and guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a pathological finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of infiltrating malignant cells within the affected bone tissue. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that characterizes سرطانة العظم, reflecting the disease’s neoplastic nature. Together, these findings underscore that both the infiltrative pattern of malignant cells and the presence of malignant tumor cells are key diagnostic features of سرطانة العظم, aiding clinicians in confirming the diagnosis and guiding appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2655
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of bone cancer. The identification of a malignant cellular infiltrate reflects the invasion of cancerous cells into bone tissue, while a secondary lesion denotes additional disease sites that arise as the primary bone tumor progresses. Recognizing these findings together aids clinicians in confirming the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم, assessing disease extent, and guiding appropriate therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2168
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary Lesion is identified as a finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the presence of secondary lesions may support the diagnosis of this bone malignancy. Conversely, Tumor cells, benign are not considered a finding of سرطانة العظم, meaning that benign tumor cells do not contribute to the identification or confirmation of this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2716
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as the abnormal cells that underlie the disease entity known as bone tumor (ورم العظام).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.
